title: The Ethics of AI Employee Clones
source: The Guardian - Beijing Review - Vol. 69 No. 17, 23 April 2026
content:
A recent controversy over companies creating digital replicas of their former employees has exposed a growing legal and ethical faultline in the use of artificial intelligence. Increasingly, businesses are utilizing AI to generate virtual clones of staff members without obtaining their explicit permission. The prevailing corporate attitude often treats employees’ digital footprints as scrap data that can be repurposed at will. However, legal experts warn that this data constitutes legally protected personal information.

For a company to create AI replicas out of such material without consent is not only likely to violate China’s Personal Information Protection Law, but it also runs against basic principles of workplace ethics. Left unchecked, this practice threatens to deepen workplace anxiety significantly. Employees may increasingly fear that they are easily replaceable by digital avatars that require no salary or benefits. Establishing clear ethical guidelines and robust legal frameworks is essential to ensure that technological advancement does not come at the expense of human dignity.
